title: Biosecurity on a smallholding
subtitle: Practical measures for limiting disease movement between holdings and visitors.
excerpt: Biosecurity is the set of practices that prevent disease entering your holding or spreading if it does. For a small holding, the highest risks are bought-in animals, shared equipment, and visitor contact. This covers the measures that make the most practical difference in a non-commercial context.
sourceNotes: DEFRA biosecurity guidance for livestock keepers. APHA, preventing disease on livestock holdings. NFU Scotland, smallholder biosecurity guide.

glossaryTerms.definition: The collective practices that prevent infectious disease from entering or spreading within a livestock holding. Includes physical barriers, quarantine procedures, disinfection, and movement controls.

A shallow tray of approved disinfectant solution placed at the entry to an animal housing area. Visitors and keepers step through it before entering. The disinfectant must be changed regularly and maintained at the correct dilution to remain effective.

content.text: The most common route for disease to enter a smallholding is bought-in animals, followed by shared equipment and visitors who have been in contact with other livestock.
does not require significant investment; it requires consistent habits.

All bought-in livestock should be quarantined for a minimum of three to four weeks on arrival, separate from existing stock. Treat for worms and external parasites at the start of quarantine. This period allows incubating diseases to present and allows observation of the animal's normal behaviour before mixing. For the pre-purchase checks that reduce the risk of buying in disease, see the companion guide on sourcing stock.
Ask visitors who have been in contact with other livestock to use a

and change footwear before entering housing areas. This includes vets and agricultural merchants. Do not borrow equipment (hurdles, trailers, feeding equipment) from other holdings without disinfecting it on arrival. Shared equipment is a well-documented route for disease transmission.

Fallen stock must be removed from the field promptly. In England, fallen stock (excluding pigs and poultry) can be collected by a licensed fallen stock collector; contact the National Fallen Stock Company for the service. Leaving dead animals in the field or burying them on a registered holding is illegal in most circumstances. Store fallen stock in a dedicated area away from housing and watercourses until collection.

Keep a visitor log for anyone entering the livestock area, particularly during disease alerts. APHA may request these records during a disease investigation. The holding register also provides the movement history needed to support disease tracing. Both are straightforward to maintain and have significant value if a disease event does occur.
